nodes.label: AI as research accelerator
nodes.label_negative: AI as sole source of information
nodes.grounding_span: use it as a starting point to follow up on and rework, while lessening some of the load

nodes.label: AI reliability for practical tasks
nodes.label_negative: AI unreliability for statistical and methodological decisions
nodes.grounding_span: I don't trust AI to say 'your data do not meet the requirements for ANOVA, therefore I recommend running a Kruskal Wallis instead'

nodes.label: AI as literature discovery tool
nodes.grounding_span: upload PDFs of several potentially related scholarly articles and asking AI to summarize or look for certain ideas

nodes.label: AI practicality of experimental designs
nodes.label_negative: AI for experimental design without human oversight
nodes.grounding_span: AI could be a powerful tool when it comes to statistics and experimental design

nodes.label: Trustworthiness and accuracy
nodes.grounding_span: I don't trust AI to say 'your data do not meet the requirements for ANOVA'

nodes.label: Scholarly judgment and critical thinking
nodes.grounding_span: I won't take anything wholly from AI, rather, use it as a starting point to follow up on and rework

nodes.label: Cautious trust in AI
nodes.grounding_span: I'm currently at the stage with AI where I won't take anything wholly from AI, rather, use it as a starting point

nodes.label: Distrust of AI reliability
nodes.grounding_span: I don't trust AI to say 'your data do not meet the requirements for ANOVA'

nodes.label: Cautious reliance on AI for experimental design
nodes.grounding_span: AI could be a powerful tool when it comes to statistics and experimental design

nodes.label: Iterative verification and follow-up
nodes.grounding_span: I would absolutely follow up on, but what is nice is the starting point

nodes.label: Manual diagnostic checking before automation
nodes.grounding_span: I usually do this manually (checking for normality, residuals vs. estimates, etc.) before running the test itself
